planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Indian pink is one of the finest native perennials for shaded gardens and is intensely attractive to ruby-throated hummingbirds.

directions: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Indian pink adapts to full shade but blooms most prolifically with 2–4 hours of direct sun or bright, dappled light. Spigelia marilandica is one of the most striking and under-used native wildflowers of eastern North American woodland borders and forest edges; it produces pairs of opposite, ovate, sessile, glossy-green leaves on upright stems 18–24 inches tall and bears terminal, one-sided clusters of tubular flowers in late spring through early summer that are brilliant scarlet-red on the outside and brilliant yellow on the inside, creating a stunning bicolor effect that is irresistible to ruby-throated hummingbirds. The plant is neat, clump-forming, relatively slow to spread, and essentially carefree once established; it is a premier choice for hummingbird gardens in zones 5–9. All parts are toxic if ingested.

planting.note: Sow direct on the soil surface 4–6 weeks before last frost; seeds need light to germinate — do not cover. Indian tobacco self-seeds freely in disturbed woodland edges and naturalizes in open, moist, partially shaded areas.

directions: Surface sow seeds on moist soil 4–6 weeks before last frost — do not cover as seeds require light for germination; Indian tobacco germinates readily in cool, moist soil and grows quickly in sun or light shade. Lobelia inflata is a native annual wildflower found in disturbed woodland edges, roadsides, and open fields across eastern North America; it produces small, pale blue to white, two-lipped flowers from mid-summer to fall followed by distinctive, inflated seed capsules that give the plant its species name. The plant has significant ethnobotanical importance as the source of the alkaloid lobeline and was historically used by Native Americans and in 19th-century botanical medicine — however, ALL PARTS ARE TOXIC, causing nausea and other adverse effects if ingested. It is grown primarily for naturalized wildflower meadow plantings and native plant restoration projects where its self-seeding habit is an asset.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; hoary skullcap is a graceful native perennial for shaded garden borders, excellent in combination with native ferns and woodland wildflowers.

directions: Sow direct in spring or start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to dappled sun; hoary skullcap tolerates more shade than most native mints and is one of the finest native perennials for shaded borders and woodland garden edges. Scutellaria incana is a tall, upright native eastern North American perennial in the mint family producing hairy, gray-green, ovate, toothed leaves with a downy, hoary appearance (giving rise to the common name) and showy, long-tubed, violet-blue flowers in branched terminal racemes in mid- to late summer — a valuable late-season blue for shaded gardens. The flowers are an important nectar source for native bumblebees and are excellent in naturalized wildflower gardens, prairie borders, and shaded native plant beds alongside native grasses, coneflowers, and ferns.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; hyssop skullcap tolerates drier, sandier conditions than most native skullcaps and is excellent for naturalized, dry, open woodland plantings.

directions: Sow direct in spring in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; hyssop skullcap tolerates poor, sandy soils and drought better than most native skullcaps and is an excellent choice for dry, open woodland naturalistic plantings in the Southeast. Scutellaria integrifolia is a native eastern US perennial skullcap of dry, open woodlands and sandy slopes producing simple, narrow, lance-shaped to oval, entire (unlobed) leaves and attractive blue-violet, tubular, two-lipped flowers with a large lower lip bearing distinctive markings in late spring and early summer. Like all skullcaps, it has a history of use in Native American and herbal medicine, but modern cultivation is primarily for its ornamental value and its role as a native pollinator plant supporting native bumblebees and long-tongued bees in dry, sunny to lightly shaded landscapes.

planting.note: Sow direct 4–6 weeks before last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; yellow giant hyssop tolerates partial shade better than most agastaches and grows vigorously along woodland edges and in open, naturalized meadows.

directions: Sow direct 4–6 weeks before last frost in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; yellow giant hyssop tolerates partial shade and adapts to a wider range of moisture conditions than purple giant hyssop, naturalizing along woodland borders and stream margins. Agastache nepetoides is the tallest of the eastern North American native giant hyssops, reaching 4–6 feet tall with large, heart-shaped, minty-scented leaves and dense, elongated spikes of tiny, pale-yellow to white flowers with protruding yellow-green bracts in mid- to late summer that attract enormous numbers of native bees, bumblebees, and butterflies. Despite the somewhat inconspicuous individual flowers, the plant's tall, vertical form and prolific blooming make it a standout in naturalized meadow and woodland-edge plantings; the seed heads persist through winter and provide food for seed-eating birds. It self-seeds moderately in favorable locations.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; sweet Joe-Pye weed is one of the tallest and most spectacular late-summer native perennials, reaching 4–7 feet tall.

directions: Sow direct in spring or start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; sweet Joe-Pye weed tolerates more shade than most large native perennials and performs well in the transition zone between sunny meadows and shaded woodland. Eutrochium purpureum (formerly Eupatorium purpureum) is a majestic native perennial of the eastern and central US, producing stout, wine-stained purple stems bearing whorls of large, coarsely toothed, vanilla-scented (when crushed) leaves and large, domed clusters of tiny, mauve-pink to rosy-purple flowers from late summer through early fall that are among the finest late-season pollinator plants available — a single mature plant can support hundreds of individual butterflies over a season, including monarchs, tiger swallowtails, and fritillaries. Its massive size (4–7 feet) makes it most appropriate at the back of borders, along pond margins, and in naturalized meadow plantings; the seed heads provide winter interest and bird food.
